content.assessment: T6 extends gatekeeping to church/private maintainers, which is fiscally significant because these providers are outside state capitation-cap jurisdiction and have grown substantially, absorbing selective demand without the same public funding-loss accountability that state-sector feeder schools face under F-live-education_finance-05. As a preventive/administrative tool it has low direct fiscal cost, but its value for money is limited to marginal future expansion — it does not redistribute existing resources to already-disadvantaged feeder schools nor address the achievement gap in F-live-education_finance-07, meaning its fiscal footprint, though efficient per action taken, is narrow in scope.
opportunities: Extending gatekeeping across all maintainer types could, if paired with funding-incidence transparency requirements, expose the true fiscal cost of church/private selective growth relative to state-sector alternatives
strengths: Low direct fiscal cost: formalizes existing discretionary administrative power rather than creating new expenditure

Closes a jurisdictional gap where church/private growth (documented separately) escapes the capitation-based accountability mechanisms that make state-sector feeder-school losses visible under F-live-education_finance-05
threats: Without accompanying compensatory funding for existing feeder-school losses, blocking future expansion does not address the resource-incidence harm already embedded in the system by capitation-based competition (F-live-education_finance-05)
weaknesses: Purely preventive scope means it cannot redress the already-existing resource losses to feeder schools from past selective expansions (F-live-education_finance-05)
No mechanism to redirect any freed-up or protected capitation resources toward the disadvantaged-achievement gap noted in F-live-education_finance-07
